comments.content:
[IMAGE:0]It's just past midnight, so here we go with a new Magic Monday. The picture is of Wilhelm Reich, Sigmund Freud's strangest and most innovative pupil, whose research into the role of sex in human psychology led him to discover the same life force that Mesmer, Reichenbach, Hieronymus, and so many others have discovered down through the years. Reich called it "orgone" because of its role in sexual orgasm, and -- again, like Mesmer, Reichenbach, and Hieronymus -- devised ways to concentrate and direct it. He was incautious enough to use it to treat illness in 20th century America, as a result of which he died in prison and his books were burnt by the US government. (Fortunately enough copies had been sold that they're still readily available today.) The moral to this story?  Don't interfere with the medical industry's stranglehold on health care in this country...at least not in public. 

Ask me anything about occultism and I'll do my best to answer it. Any question received by midnight Monday Eastern time will get an answer, though I'm going to have somewhat limited email access tomorrow and it may be Tuesday or even Wednesday before I get to them all. If you're in a hurry, or suspect you may be the 143,916th person to ask a question, please check out the very rough version 1.0 of The Magic Monday FAQ here.

I've had several people ask about tipping me for answers here, and though I certainly don't require that I won't turn it down. You can use the button below to access my online tip jar.  

With that said, have at it!

***This Magic Monday is now closed -- and yes, that means you. See you next week!***

comments.title: Geomancy
comments.content:
I have a question on "conflicting" meanings between the overall message of a geomantic reading and the message of certain figures in it. What I have in mind is something like this: "Will XY turn out positive for me". The chart will perfect in one or more ways, but the figure for the querent is not favorable. To give a concrete example:

I asked a question about a certain situation in my working place and whether my approach to solve it will turn out positive for me. The most relavant figures were 1st and 9th: cauda draconis, 7th, 10th and 12th laetitia, rw: conjunctio, lw: amissio, j: fortuna minor. From the nature of the questions it seems plausible to treat this both as a question for 10 or 7th house. Either way the chart perfects - by conjunction and mutation of by a double conjunction. Additionally there are aspects involved, some fortunate, some not.

The most straightforward interpretation I can think of is something along these lines "Yes, overall it's a very good thing to do so! Still, there are challenging aspects to it and it might involve you giving up on something or stop to do something, etc". The "counter-chart" on what would happen if I do nothing did not perfect and had unfavorable figures for querent and quesited, btw.

As always, thank you very much for your time and energy, MMs are a great source of information!
Nachtgurke

replies.title: Re: Geomancy
replies.content:
Yes, that's very much the way I'd read it: the results will certainly be positive, but you will have to stop doing something or give something up in the process of making it happen. The left witness also supports that interpretation.

As a rule, I find that the presence or absence of a mode of perfection gives you your yes/no answer, and the characters in the house of the querent and quesited, and the character making a translation if there is one, tell you what will be involved in making it happen.

If so, that's considered a highly auspicious symbol in many traditions -- for example, it's one of 65 auspicious symbols in Buddhism, and a common emblem of the goddess Kali in Hindu tradition. replies.title: (no subject)

If the Nazi swastika had been in the usual orientation the regime might not have expanded so explosively and then collapsed so quickly.

replies.title: Re: The solar system
replies.content: Well, occultism isn't astrophysics, to begin with. (There's a lot of old occult philosophy that's burdened with outdated science!) So what you'll find in Theosophy should be read as metaphor rather than physical prediction.

comments.title: Affirmation
comments.content:
Last week I read the affirmations post when the discussion had already moved on, so I would like to ask a question now. I knew almost immediately after reading the post what I wanted to work on in in affirmation, and I also accepted that it would take hundreds of repetitions for a year in order to work. My doubt is whether the affirmation is prudent and ethical. What came immediately into my mind was “[My wife’s name] and I desire each other”.

Is it a good idea in this case to affirm something that also depends on another person’s free will?

The background is that when we met each other, I was both amazed and a bit intimidated to date and then to move in with a stunningly beautiful woman, who also had more varied sexual experience than I. Since she is not somebody to make or to appreciate pretty words, I kind of convinced myself that she liked me well enough as a person and that in spite of not feeling particularly physically attracted to me, her sex drive was strong enough to make it work for both of us. Years later, she told me that she did, in fact, find certain physical aspects of me attractive at the beginning of our relationship, though she would never have dreamt of telling me so at the time.

In any case, our sex life came to a rather abrupt end when our daughter was born several years ago. My now wife passed through clinical depression, postpartum and other, and though things are better now, sex continues rare and meagre. She tells me she feels no erotic attraction towards anybody at all, and I have every reason to believe her. Otherwise, I feel loved, and in spite of frustration, I still very much love her and find her the most attractive woman on earth.

All this is to say, my intention is to behave as if the affirmation were true, not to somehow force her to feel or do anything, and my hope is that by behaving with more self-assurance, she might rediscover her desire. Do you think this is a wise affirmation?

On considering various fictional accounts of people who desire, but hurt each other terribly, I decided to amend the words to “… desire and love each other”. I have used this affirmation for almost a week now and feel quite encouraged by some changes, but if you consider it ill-placed, I will reconsider.

replies.title: Re: Affirmation
replies.content: Have you considered talking to her and asking if she's willing to have you do that? Consent is as important in magic as it is in sex -- and it becomes absolutely essential when the two of them are combined.

replies.title: Re: Affirmation
replies.content:
Not original poster

If his wife says it is ok to go ahead with this will/could this work? It seems to me that this affirmation is trying to change something outside of the posters subconscious. If I understand correctly how these things are supposed to work this affirmation won’t work. Am I correct? And if I am correct what kind of affirmation could help in this situation?

Thanks again JMG

replies.title: Re: Affirmation
replies.content: Given consent, yes, it can work. OP may be doing things subconsciously that feed into the situation with his wife; furthermore, when two people are in a close consensual relationship, changes in the subconscious of one person can flow over into the other's subconscious and have an effect there. The sort of affirmation I'd use in a situation like this would focus on both partners: for example, something like "my wife and I have frequent satisfying sex with each other."

replies.title: Re: Affirmation
replies.content:
Just a thought. If you want the best for her, and it sounds like you do, after asking her permission of course, maybe restate it as "My wife and I are genuinely fulfilled by each other".

I am married nearly 20 years now and I love my husband, but I am almost completely uninterested in sex. I'm nearer to 50 than 40. My drive fell off about six years ago and never returned. No kids for us by choice. He is fourteen years older than I am and though we are very affectionate and loving towards each other, we don't get amorous more than once every few months.

Every marriage is different because every person is different. I hope you guys are able to figure out what makes you happiest.

replies.content:
If I may suggest considering (and reproduce a spell from a book - but according to the book's author, the spell is *extremely* "out of copyright"):

(not meant as *in place of* relevant affirmation; "1966 CE" notwithstanding, I don't mean it as a suggestion to join the Temple of Set either)

"III. From a Coptic Grimoire
Oil Spell for Sealing a Marriage with Hot Sex
Spell for mutual love between a man and a woman

Text: Michigan 4932f
Description: vellum, 5 1/2 x 15 1/4 in., "probably fairly early" (see Worrell)
Bibliography: William H. Worrell, "Coptic Magical and Medical Texts," 184-87

To be said over massage oil:

Oil! Oil! Oil! Holy oil!

Oil that flows from under the throne of Yao Sabaoth! Oil with which Isis anointed Osiris' bones! I call you, oil. The sun and moon call you. The stars of heaven call you. The servants of the sun call you. I want to send you. You must come so that I may bring you and you may bring [name of target] daughter of [name of target's mother] to me - me, [name of magician] son of [name of magician's mother] - and you must make my love be in her heart and hers in mine like a brother and sister, or a bear who wants to suckle her young. Yea, yea, I invoke you, the one whose head is in heaven, whose feet are in the abyss, before whom is what is also under the Sheep, behind whom is what is also under Draco, the one before whom the heaven of all darkness is hung, the Holy Headless One who encircles all and who speaks to those who rise to his level in truth.
I shall uproot him by iron. I shall melt him away. No, my lord, do not hand me over to Dimelouchs, who judges. Instead I want you to descend to hell and uproot all thoughts of the devil about [name of target] child of [name of target's mother], and make my love be in her heart and hers in mine. For it is I who invoke: it is you who fulfill the desire.

Note: This spell appeared in a Coptic magical book. Set, he who is behind Draco, cannot be named in this Christian text. This spell appears unique in that it is designed for re-creating Love and sex in a marriage and focusing the energies of both parties *willingly* and *consciously* away from other objects of desire. A powerful spell for our age - which clearly led into the Christian tradition of anointing oils.
Here is the first example of the preconscious - the Egyptian Duat - replaced by the Greek Tartarus (Hell) - and the devil banished to this region. This spell shows Set-Sia, who fights Apep so that the right thoughts may manifest at the right time, making his last stand as the destroyer of illusion (rather than its creator). In 1966 CE he was called back. If you lack a good source of oils and incense in your area, you might try 12th House Arts & Publishing, POB 1012, Monitowoc, WI 54220. They've always done good work for me."

(The emphasis I reproduced was italics in the original.)

Source: The Seven Faces of Darkness (! - as the author does say, this spell is unique in it, though others within don't sound particularly shady - and the book has non-spell things that don't seem ethically-challenged).

JMG:
1) what do you think about it? In particular would you consider something in similar spirit with different text a better idea?
(the folowing if the line of inquiry has any merit, as per 1).)
2) it says "love", never "sex" - do you think using it by/with/for people who do love each other - without sex - would be damaging/useless?
3) Set is represented and pronouns are male-perspective - do you think there'd be anything non-obvious to a woman using it?
4) for someone with this interest doing a relevant affirmation, do you think it'd be a good idea to ask *the other person* to do this (as a form of collaboration less demanding than long-term affirmation), provided they actually like the idea?
5) for someone with this interest and a crafty spouse, do you think it'd be a good idea to (again, if *good* will is present) to get the spouse to make the oil and do such a spell oneself?

(If unclear, 4 and 5 are the point I most wanted to make.)

- S. T. Silva.

comments.title: Remedy against projection
comments.content:
Dear Archdruid,

Your posts on the Kek Wars brought up this question: I get the impression that I regularly tend to soak up negative projections from groups of people, and partly believing, even slightly acting on them. This happens on top of my personal mistakes and weaknesses which I’m not trying to deny here. I just notice that my assumptions about myself change according to the group I’m with. Maybe that’s regular group dynamics, or negative self-talk, or maybe a habit of picking up and identifying with shadows.

Sometimes similar things happen in my romantic relationships. I used to attribute this to my Pluto placement in the first house, forming an exact T-square with Moon and Mercury.

I’m already using regular banishings and most of the other tools you recommend, so I wanted to ask if you had a specific advice for this kind of problem?

Thank you as always for sharing your wisdom!

replies.title: Re: Remedy against projection
replies.content:
That's actually normal; you might want to read up on what psychologists call the 'Pygmalion effect.' Unless they're on the autism spectrum -- which means they don't have the 'mirror neurons' that make this happen -- human beings get much of their self-image and most of their emotional reactions from the other people they're with.

Since I have Aspergers syndrome (which is an autism spectrum disorder) I may not be the best person to ask about this! That said, regular banishings and daily meditation will gradually give you the capacity to stand aside from what, in occult terms, is a phenomenon of the lower astral plane. In the meantime, positive affirmations can definitely help.

replies.title: Re: Remedy against projection
replies.content: Thank you. Why could it be then that I pick up on this more easily than other people? Would a talisman be helpful as well?

replies.title: Re: Remedy against projection
replies.content: You may be etherically or astrally sensitive. A talisman or amulet might help -- in your place I'd check your natal chart and see if your Sun is well dignified, and if so, a solar talisman or amulet would be a good choice.

replies.content: And I’m very much looking forward to your post on the lower astral - or did I miss it?

replies.title: Re: Remedy against projection

Each of us is constantly given chances to participate in the karma of others. What we do in response to those chances shapes our own karma. The law of the conservation of karma would actually make a lot of sense; there's a certain amount of suffering that everyone goes through in the course of their human lives, and if there's a fixed amount of negative karma and you get to deal with a share of it, well, there you are.

Most of what I've seen that deals with karma is very specifically from a Hindu or Buddhist standpoint, and their conception is rather different from that of Western occultism. The Western idea is simply that every action has its consequences, and those consequences work through character: "Sow a thought and reap an action; sow an action and reap a habit; sow a habit and reap a character; sow a character and reap a destiny." There's no outside force passing judgment -- it's just that what you do affects who you are, and who you are has a huge influence on what happens to you.

replies.title: Re: Democrat nominees getting ill on 9/11
replies.content:
Okay, first of all, remember that Clinton and Biden are both very, very old. Not that many years ago, it would have been out of the question for either of them to have run for president at their age -- it's only because the Boomers are clinging to power long after their time is up that we have so many geriatric politicians these days. Running for office is very stressful, and so it's no wonder that they had health problems!

Secondly, fame as such doesn't protect against magical attack. If you have a vast number of people beaming positive thoughts and hopes and prayers your way, that's a very solid protection, especially if that's being done in a religious context -- one of the reasons Trump has proven so durable, old as he is, is that he's got millions of conservative Christians and Jews praying for his welfare every single day. Clinton and Biden don't have that advantage -- their followers don't tend to be devout -- and Biden, certainly, hasn't attracted the kind of passionate enthusiasm that would help.

So if you're going to be a public figure of the kind that attracts political hatred, make sure that the policies you support are popular with at least one group of people who will pray for you good and hard -- and actually do things to benefit your constituents, rather than simply going through the motions and expecting them to love you anyway.

replies.title: Re: Affirmations
replies.content:
It's not general and personal, it's general and specific -- both affirmations, and all affirmations generally, are personal in focus.

The division between general and specific affirmations is a thing that some New Thought systems taught back in the day. You had a general affirmation that was oriented toward broad general spiritual goals -- for example, "I am one with the One Life of the universe; it flows through me to accomplish all my worthy desires" or "I breathe in power from the divine source of all power; I send it forth to accomplish my worthy and constructive desires." (Note the common thread here: power flows in from the source, power flows out to all positive desires. This was very common back in the day.) You say that every morning, year in, year out, to give your life in general the positive tone you want.

Your specific affirmation targets some more narrowly defined issue: say, a desire for greater economic success. That's laser focused on the issue you have in mind: "I recognize opportunities to prosper and succeed all around me," or what have you. You say it often, and follow through on it as appropriate. When your finances are great and you're ready to push in another direction, you set that affirmation aside and start working with another.

2) I recommend using any affirmation for at least a year and a day, so it has time to work. Other than that, yes.

replies.title: Re: Divinations and talismans

comments.title: Practicum
comments.content:
Not a question: just had to share this; I'm on a business trip right now, and given a five-hour flight to get here, I decided to finally dig in and finish Prof. Ioan Culianu's famous text _Eros and Magic in the Renaissance_. (I started a year or more ago and got about a third through and then life intervened; we moved house.) It was as eye-opening and thought-provoking as I hoped. I have a completely different understanding now of what the Renaissance actually _was_, which has made much of your reading of that history seem much clearer to me. Makes me wonder if anyone has written a similar intellectual history of the so-called "12th Century Renaissance".

That was yesterday. Now I am at my business event in the city of-- wait for it-- Las Vegas. And yes, on the Vegas Strip, America's 24-hour all-you-can-eat cacomagic buffet.

I feel like a student in the sciences who just spent an hour in the lecture hall with a brilliant professor and is now walking into the lab, ready to apply new insights.

Of course it doesn't take an Ioan Culianu to notice that Las Vegas is absolutely bat$;!* crazy, or to see why. But having learnt a bit from him, it's much easier to see exactly _how it's done_. I guess I shouldn't be surprised, but it turns out that it doesn't make it any more pleasant or easier to endure. Now to go to my first meetings, to do which I will have to walk past hundreds of slot machines and gambling tables, many in full use at nine in the morning; time for some extra-credit work!

replies.title: Re: Practicum
replies.content:
Oh dear gods, yes. The first time I ever went to Vegas -- I was there as an invited speaker at an alchemy conference, if you like ironies -- I studied the scene in the casino hotel, and realized that I'd finally encountered an experience that made the Christian concept of Hell believable. The whole place was full of people caught up in a mindless trance of frustrated greed, unable to leave, unable to realize what they were doing, feeding endless streams of money into slot machines and getting just enough back to keep them pinned there. That was pretty clearly the fourth circle of Dante's Hell, where the avaricious suffer an eternal punishment that is simply the endless recycling of their own twisted desires.

I don't know of a study of the 12th century that does the same thing as Couliano's book, but it would be very good to have one!

comments.title: Occult significance of Seven Sages
comments.content:
Hi, JMG. Thanks, as always, for being so generous with your time and hosting these Magic Mondays every week!
It just so happened that I was recently reading the Hindu account of the global deluge and noted that the boat which saved the Vedas from the flood also accommodated the Seven Sages (“saptarishis” in Sanskrit). Not too long back I was reading some research on Atlantis in which the researcher noted that in many cultures’ legends of the global flood (but not the biblical account) there is mention of seven (always seven) effulgent sages who gave wisdom to the survivors of the flood. Fascinating in its own right, but I would like to dwell on the possible occult meaning. To me, “seven” indicates the classical planets. Are you aware of any other occult meaning of there being “seven” sages who teach humanity?

replies.title: Re: Occult significance of Seven Sages
replies.content:
An interesting take on the significance of 7 in our culture and its roots is found in Geoffrey Ashe's Dawn Behind the Dawn (Henry Holt & Co Inc, NYC, 1992 ISBN 0-8050-1070X (alk. paper) Hardback. This book goes into a vast number of things, including a different take on Apollo and Artemis (She Who Was A Bear) and a possible source for Y-Houndeh the Elk Goddess (did whichever pulp writer who created her know much about circumpolar shamanistic religions?) Wild, weird, wonderful, plausible in many areas... a true find.

this is a first edition: all Henry Holt's first editions are (or were in 1992) printed on acid-free paper.

Pat, from her own bookshelves.

replies.title: Re: Occult significance of Seven Sages
replies.content: With regard to Yhoundeh and the writer Clark Ashton Smith who invented her, that's a good question -- he didn't say much about the cult of Yhoundeh and never described her. The material I invented for The Weird of Hali, on the other hand, drew on a fair amount of knowledge of circumpolar shamanism, and also a good bit of Greek mythology!

replies.title: Re: Occult significance of Seven Sages
replies.content: Yes, and the seven sages are usually associated with the seven stars of Ursa Major -- iirc that's the traditional association of the saptarishi. Joscelyn Godwin's fine book Arktos discusses the polar tradition in occult symbolism and would be a good starting place.

replies.title: Re: Occult significance of Seven Sages
replies.content:
Thanks for the book reference, JMG. You are correct about the saptarishi and Ursa Major (with Dhruva, the great devotee of Vishnu, as Polaris in the Hindu tradition).

Ron M

replies.title: Re: Occult significance of Seven Sages

comments.title: Geomancy: House vs. Shield
comments.content:
Dear Archdruid,

There was a Facebook forum discussion about geomancy last week, about interpreting a shield chart as if it were a house chart (i.e, treating the first niece as if it were the ninth house, or the eighth house as if it were the fourth daughter). I said something to the effect that I thought you'd indicated here at one point that it wasn't a good idea to interpret house charts as if they were shield charts, or vice versa, and that this often led to 'gobbledegook interpretations.'

Several people said that they thought that this didn't sound like you, and several others vaguely remembered you saying something similar but not the same as what I'd said... and none of us could find it.

Could you clarify your position on this, either "cast a new chart when shifting between house and shield for the same question" vs. "simply convert the symbols from the first type of chart to the second", or a third option? And is there a "why?" behind the answer?

replies.title: Re: Geomancy: House vs. Shield
replies.content: In my experience, it works best to decide which way you're going to interpret the chart before you cast it, and then stick with that. Otherwise people tend to shop for the answer they want! So make up your mind in advance; if you decide to do a shield chart, take the witnesses and judge as your answer; if you decide to do a house chart, take the presence or absence of a mode of perfection as your answer; and then accept the answer you get even if it's not the answer you want. ;-)

comments.title: Sphere of Protection
comments.content: So I've now moved onto Phase Two of the Sphere of Protection. I'm working within a Catholic tradition, and I feel uncomfortable invoking anything other than the Holy Trinity, or anything not approved by the Holy Trinity. Is it okay if I remove references to "Air" and replace it with Jesus?

replies.title: Re: Sphere of Protection
replies.content:
No, if you're not going to work with the elements at all you're not going to get any of the benefits out of the practice. What you might want to do instead is to assign an angel or a saint to each of the seven stations, and make it clear from the wording that you're treating the elements as creatures of God, not as deities. For example: "In the name of the Holy Trinity and with the help of St. Raphael the Archangel I call upon the energies of air. With the Divine permission, may they bless and protect me this day and always. And in the name of Jesus and by all the blessed energies of air I banish from within me and around me and from all my doings all harmful influences and every snare of Satan. I banish these far from me."

A suitable Catholic set of correspondences is as follows:

Air: St. Raphael the Archangel
Water: St. Gabriel the Archangel
Fire: St. Michael the Archangel
Earth: St. Uriel the Archangel
Spirit Below: the Blessed Virgin Mother
Spirit Above: the Risen Christ

I don't think you'll find any theological improprieties there!

comments.title: Reincarnation
comments.content:
Hello! I have two questions:

1) What role do the Ancestors play in a reincarnation model? For instance, if someone were to make offerings and petitions to a former incarnation of me, how would that affect my current incarnation? What about deified dead?

2) One could concievably make the accusation that, in light of a reincarnation model, we shouldn't help those that suffer because they're simply getting what they deserve. That abuse victims and the like are being "punished for their sins" in a past life and we shouldn't interfere. How would you respond?

Thanks!

replies.title: Re: Reincarnation
replies.content:
1) Some of your ancestors have already completed their sequence of human incarnations and are active on the inner realms, on levels above the human. Some of the others have not yet entered into a new incarnation. Both categories can respond to prayers. This is why in many societies that practice ancestor veneration -- for example, in Japan -- you make offerings to individual dead for a fixed period, and the bulk of the offerings go to "the ancestors" in general -- after the fixed period, the idea is that your ancestor is back in incarnation.

2) That's a very common misconception. You don't know what somebody who suffers actually deserves; how do you know, for example, that they don't deserve to be helped by you? At each moment, furthermore, you're making your own karma; if you refuse to help someone you could help, that karma will affect you in nasty ways in your next life.

comments.content:
"Now draw your joined hands down the front of your body to touch a point on your belly just below your navel, at the location of the womb center." I want to be sure I'm understanding you: which way are your fingers pointing in this part of the SOP opening? I can't keep my palms together unless I rotate my hands about 90 degrees outward.

Thanks!

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Don't join your palms. Rest one set of fingers over the other in a broad upside-down V, palms facing you, so that the tips of your index fingers touch. Now pivot your fingers over each other, with the palms still facing you, so that the tips of your pinkies either touch or point toward each other, and the V points downward. Does that make sense?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: I'm afraid I'm still pretty lost. (And now I'm staring at my hands as if I've never seen them before.) A picture or diagram would be fantastic.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
I don't have a diagram handy, so let's do it a step at a time.

Rest your right hand, palm up and fingers together, on a table or desk in front of you. Move it so that it's in front of your belly, and slants across the table or desk like this / .

Now take your right hand, also palm up, and lay it across your left hand so that the fingers of the right hand are on top of the fingers of your left. Your right hand slants this way \ . The fingers cross each other at right angles. The nail of your right index finger rests on the pad of your left index finger. Your left pinkie is under the knuckles of your right hand.

Got it? Now raise your hands together, without changing their position, and press the inside of your right fingers to your forehead. That's the first position.

Now bring your hands down in front of your body, keeping your palms facing your body. Let the fingers slide over each other as the hands rotate, but keep the inside of your left fingers against the backs of your right fingers as they turn. Touch your belly. That's the second position. How's that?

replies.content: Aha! Got it. Thanks very much!
